session.assistant: Now let me verify by grepping for bare "Layer [1-5]" references in the skill files to make sure none were missed:
session.tool_result: 61:1. **Layer 4 "logic" testing** - Tests business logic over fakes (70% of tests should be Layer 4 "logic")

28:- **Pure functions** test at Layer 3 "pure" (unit) with zero dependencies — no tmp_path, no CliRunner, no mocking. These cover the combinatorial edge cases that would be painful to set up through the full pipeline. 29:- **Integration entry point** tests at Layer 4 "logic" with CliRunner and `ErkContext.for_test()`. These verify the orchestration wiring — that the right pure functions are called in the right order with the right I/O.

.claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:16:│ └─> Layer 4 "logic": tests/unit/ or tests/services/ (over fakes) ← START HERE .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:20:│ └─> Layer 3 "pure": tests/unit/ (pure unit tests, no fakes/mocks) .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:24:│ └─> Layer 1 "fake-check": tests/unit/fakes/test_fake_*.py .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:28:│ └─> Layer 2 "real-sanity": tests/integration/test_real_*.py .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:32: └─> Layer 5 "smoke": tests/e2e/ (integration tests, sparingly) .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:38:- For business logic with dependencies → Layer 4 "logic" (tests over fakes) .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:39:- For pure utilities with no dependencies → Layer 3 "pure" (pure unit tests) .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:80:│ ├── fakes/ ← Tests OF fakes (Layer 1 "fake-check") .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:84:│ ├── test_string_utils.py ← Pure unit tests (Layer 3 "pure") .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:85:│ ├── test_parsers.py ← Pure unit tests (Layer 3 "pure") .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:86:│ ├── services/ ← Business logic tests (Layer 4 "logic") .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:93:├── integration/ ← Integration sanity tests (Layer 2 "real-sanity") .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:94:│ ├── test_real_database.py ← Layer 2 "real-sanity": mocked connections .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:97:├── e2e/ ← Business logic integration tests (Layer 5 "smoke") Layer 1 "fake-check": Fake tests | 1-2 tests | Verify `FakeAuthService.authenticate()` tracks correctly | .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:495:| Layer 2 "real-sanity": tests | Test password hashing, token generation logic | .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:497:| Layer 4 "logic": Business logic | 12-14 tests | Test auth flow over fakes (success, failures, edge cases) | .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:498:| Layer 5 "smoke": Integration tests | 1 test | Smoke test complete login flow | .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/quick-reference.md:500:**Total**: ~20 tests, with 70% over fakes (Layer 4 "logic"), 10% pure unit (Layer 3 "pure"), 10% sanity (Layer 2 "real-sanity"), 5% integration (Layer 5 "smoke"), 5% fake tests (Layer 1 "fake-check"). .claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/testing-strategy.md:16:│ Layer 5 "smoke": Business Logic Integration Tests (5%) │ ← session.assistant: Every single "Layer N" reference now includes a short name. All results show `Layer N "short-name"` format. Let me also verify that no docs/learned files that use "Layer N" for non-testing concepts were touched:
session.tool_result:
docs/learned/testing/hook-testing.md:28:- **Pure functions** test at Layer 3 "pure" (unit) with zero dependencies — no tmp_path, no CliRunner, no mocking. These cover the combinatorial edge cases that would be painful to set up through the full pipeline. docs/learned/testing/hook-testing.md:29:- **Integration entry point** tests at Layer 4 "logic" with CliRunner and `ErkContext.for_test()`. These verify the orchestration wiring — that the right pure functions are called in the right order with the right I/O. docs/learned/testing/cli-testing.md:61:1. **Layer 4 "logic" testing** - Tests business logic over fakes (70% of tests should be Layer 4 "logic") docs/learned/testing/artifact-distribution-sync.md:23:Layer 1: Frontmatter Validation docs/learned/testing/artifact-distribution-sync.md:27:Layer 2: On-Disk Inventory docs/learned/testing/artifact-distribution-sync.md:33:Layer 3: Distribution Consistency docs/learned/reviews/tripwires.md:15:**adding a new untestable file category** → Read [Test Coverage Review Agent](test-coverage-agent.md) first. New categories must align with the 5-layer test architecture. Only files in Layers 0-2 qualify. If the file contains any business logic (Layer 3+), it requires tests regardless of how thin the logic appears. docs/learned/reviews/test-coverage-agent.md:11: warning: "New categories must align with the 5-layer test architecture. Only files in Layers 0-2 qualify. If the file contains any business logic (Layer 3+), it requires tests regardless of how thin the logic appears." docs/learned/reviews/test-coverage-agent.md:29:| Layer 1 — Thin CLI | Click-decorated functions that only delegate | Testing these would test Click, not erk | docs/learned/reviews/test-coverage-agent.md:30:| Layer 2 — Type definitions | `TypeVar`, `Protocol`, type aliases, ABCs with only abstract methods | No runtime behavior to verify | docs/learned/reviews/test-coverage-agent.md:32:Everything at Layer 3 (business logic) and Layer 4 (operations/orchestration) requires tests — the agent flags files in these layers that lack corresponding test files. docs/learned/objectives/objective-create-workflow.md:21:### Layer 1: Skill Template (`.claude/commands/erk/objective-create.md`) docs/learned/objectives/objective-create-workflow.md:36:### Layer 2: Exec Commands docs/learned/objectives/objective-create-workflow.md:46:### Layer 3: `create_objective_issue()` (`packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/github/objective_issues.py`) docs/learned/planning/branch-name-inference.md:36:**Layer 1 — Metadata lookup**: Read `branch_name` from the `plan-header` block. This succeeds when `impl-signal started` ran successfully. docs/learned/planning/branch-name-inference.md:38:**Layer 2 — Git context inference** (removed): Previously, if `branch_name` was missing, the system checked whether the current git branch matched the `P{issue_number}-` prefix. This fallback was removed in PR #8269 (which deleted `get_branch_issue()`). Resolution now relies solely on Layer 1 metadata lookup and `plan-ref.json`. docs/learned/planning/branch-name-inference.md:40:If Layer 1 does not produce a match, the command fails with an actionable error. docs/learned/planning/branch-name-inference.md:75:This was previously an instance of the [fail-open pattern](../architecture/fail-open-patterns.md) with defense-in-depth. The Layer 2 fallback (pattern matching `P{issue_number}-` prefixes against git context) was removed in PR #8269. Resolution now relies on: docs/learned/planning/reliability-patterns.md:43:**Key insight:** Only the dedicated workflow step is truly reliable. Upstream layers reduce the frequency of Layer 3 needing to act, but they cannot replace it. This mirrors the broader [defense-in-depth enforcement](../architecture/defense-in-depth-enforcement.md) pattern. docs/learned/planning/reliability-patterns.md:55:See the "Clean up .erk/impl-context/ after implementation" step in `.github/workflows/plan-implement.yml` for the production implementation of Layer 3. docs/learned/planning/worktree-cleanup.md:73:**Only Layer 3 is deterministic.** Layers 1 and 2 reduce how often Layer 3 needs to act, but cannot replace it. docs/learned/planning/worktree-cleanup.md:78:See the "Clean up .erk/impl-context/ after implementation" step in `.github/workflows/plan-implement.yml` for the production Layer 3 implementation. docs/learned/planning/session-deduplication.md:32:### Layer 1: Hook Blocking docs/learned/planning/session-deduplication.md:45:### Layer 2: Command-Level Deduplication docs/learned/claude-code/skill-composition-patterns.md:57:**Layer 1 (Command):** Orchestrates git workflow (stage, diff, commit, push, PR creation). At commit message generation, loads `erk-diff-analysis`. docs/learned/claude-code/skill-composition-patterns.md:59:**Layer 2 (Analysis Skill):** Loads `commit-message-prompt.md` reference and applies its principles. Returns formatted commit message to parent. docs/learned/claude-code/skill-composition-patterns.md:61:**Layer 3 (Reference):** Pure prompt text defining format, rules, anti-patterns. docs/learned/architecture/defense-in-depth-enforcement.md:71:| Layer 1 (agent) | Ideal—lowest cost | None needed | docs/learned/architecture/defense-in-depth-enforcement.md:72:| Layer 2 (skill) | Good—caught pre-PR | None needed | docs/learned/architecture/defense-in-depth-enforcement.md:73:| Layer 3 (PR check) | Acceptable—safety net working | Consider improving upstream layers |
... [40 lines omitted] ...
docs/learned/architecture/fail-open-patterns.md:141:1. **Layer 1: Lenient handler** — returns `None` on ANY failure (missing data, API errors, not found). No exceptions, no error messages. Pure gateway logic.
session.assistant:

All other `docs/learned/` files use "Layer N" for non-testing architectural concepts (defense-in-depth, parameter threading, etc.) and were correctly left untouched.
Let me also check `gateway-abc-implementation.md` — lines 672-673 reference "Layer 5 (Business Logic Integration Tests)" and "Layer 4 (Business Logic Tests)" which are testing layer references. Let me check if those should be updated:
session.tool_result:
668→## Integration with Fake-Driven Testing 669→ 670→This pattern aligns with the [Fake-Driven Testing Architecture](../testing/): 671→ 672→- **Real**: Layer 5 (Business Logic Integration Tests) - production implementation 673→- **Fake**: Layer 4 (Business Logic Tests) - in-memory test double for fast tests 674→- **DryRun**: Preview mode for CLI operations 675→ 676→## ABC Method Removal Pattern 677→
<system-reminder> Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>
